Fern Polrrrcal PnecrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
March 2,2004
Robert D. Herrick, City Attorney
City of Moreno Valley
Office of the CityAttomey
Post Office Box 88005
Moreno Valley, CA 92552-0805
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-04-019
Dear Mr. Herrick:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').r
QUESTIONS
l. May you, as city attorneS render legal advice to the city council or staff
regarding the negotiation, drafting, and hearing process for the Moreno Highlands
Project, located about one-quarter mile from your residence?
2. May you advise the council at public hearings regarding modifications to the
land use entitlements for the phase of the project near your residence or for the Highlands
Project?
CONCLUSIONS
1-2. Yes. Yourprincipal residence is located more than 500 feet from the
proposed boundaries of the Highland Project. It is presumed under the Commission's
regulations, therefore, that neither of these decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on the economic interest of your principal residence. As long as
this presumption is not rebutted, you will not have a conflict of interest concerning either
of these decisions, based on your principal residence.
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
iq9-5:


File No. 4-04-019
Page No. 2
FACTS
The City of Moreno Valley is a rapidly growing city of approximately 152,000 in
population. It is approximately 50 to 60 percent developed, with large areas of
undeveloped land on the city's east side. Your personal residence is located in a
developed single-family tract surrounded by undeveloped fields. It is located slightly
more than one-quarter of a mile from Redlands Blvd., the major north/south arterial street
in the area.
The land near your residential development contains a few large lot residential
homes, a horse ranch, and undeveloped fields that have been 'odry-farmed" for a number
of years. Most of the land has received land use entitlements as part of a specific plan
ar'td a development agreement both approved prior to your appointment as city attorney
and your purchase of your home. The development pdect, called "Moreno Highlands"
("Highlands Project') was subject to CEQA litigation at the time of its approval, and the
environmental documentation was ruled to be legally adequate. However, the project has
been dormant through the recessionary years.
The master developer for the Highlands Project wishes to resurrect the project and
make some changes to the specific plan, including the elimination of some land recently
acquired by the State of California for additional wildlife preserve and adding some land
recently acquired by the developer on the northeast corner of the project. He also wishes
to reduce the density and total number of residential units, phase the project and amend
and restate the development agreernent for the project.
The Highlands project is an irregularly shaped area roughly l% miles by 2 miles
in area. The project comprises a significant percentage (perhaps 10 to 15 percent) of the
total land area of the city and perhaps 30 to 40 percent of the undeveloped, but
developable, land in the city. Under the already approved specific plan, approximately
12,000 residential dwelling units are approved for the project. The project site is
bounded, approximately, by Redlands Blvd. to the west, the Highway 60 freeway to the
north, Gilman Springs Road to the east and the San Jacinto Wildlife preserve,
undevelopable rock hills and Cactus Blvd. to the south. Within a one-mile radius of the
southwest corner of the project there are several tracts ofhomes on lots ranging from
7,500 square feet to one acre within an older area known as "Old Moreno" and a newer
development around the Moreno Valley Ranch Golf course.
Your tract of homes is located approximately one-half mile north of the northern
edge of Old Moreno. To the west of your tract, is largely undeveloped land and rock
hills. Approximately one mile west-northwest of your hact of homes is the Moreno
Valley Auto Mall, a freeway-oriented complex of several automobile dealerships located
at the Moreno Beach Blvd. exit from the 60 Freeway. It is surrounded by orange groves
already entitled for single-family development and by undeveloped commercial property.
A "Super WalMart" has been approved fbr the city directly west of the auto mall. The
land north of your tract of the 60 Freeway is zoned commercial but currently contains a


File No. 4-04-019
Page No. 3
few scattered large lot single-family homes. Most of the land north of the 60 Freeway
from the auto mall east to the city limits is undeveloped, but zoned, for commercial and
large lot residential (one-half to one acre lots).
The developer's proposed Phase I of the development plan is for residential units
and some commercial development in the area bordered by Redlands Blvd. on the west -
the part of the development nearest your home. It would involve widening Redlands
Blvd. and developing the current horse ranch and vacant fields across Redlands Blvd.
from your housing tract- the nearest edge of Phase I being a little over one mile from
your property line.
The Highlands plan, either in its already approved configuration, or in the
proposed amended configuration, will change the area east of your home from largely
rural to largely residential.
The developerhas requested a series of meetings with city staffleading up to
planning commission and city council hearings on the amended development agreement
and specific plan. The city manager has requested the advice and counsel of the city
attorney in negotiating the proposed terms of these amended documents prior to staffs
recofirmendation to those bodies.
AI[ALYSIS
General RuIe
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported the,rn." (Section 810010).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the governmental decision
will have amaterial financial effect on one ormore ofthe official's economic interests.
(Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step
anallical framework for deciding whether a public official has a disqualifiring conflict of
interest in a given governmental decision. (Regulation 18700(b).) We apply this analysis
to your question.
1. Step One: Are you considered a o'public official"?
Section 87103 indicates that apublic official has a financial interest in a decision
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of his or her economic
interests. As the city attorney, you are a "mernber, officer, employee or consultant of a


FileNo. A-04-019
Page No. 4
state or local government agency'' and therefore, are a public official subject to the Act's
conflict of interest provisions. (Section 82048; regulation 18701(a).)
2. Step Two: Are you making participating in making or influencing a
governmental decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87L}};regulation 18702.1.) A public
official "participates in making a govemmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review,
the official negotiates, advises or makes recofllmendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87100; regulation 18702.2.) A public
official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a decision before his
or her own agency if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears
before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Section
871 00; regulation 18702.3.)
Your letter indicates that you will give advice and counsel in negotiating the
proposed terms of the amended development agreement and specific plan for the Moreno
Highlands Project prior to the staffs recorlmendation to the planning commission and the
city council. These actions constitute participation in making a govemmental decision.
Additionally, if you engage in any of the actions detailed above with regard to a decision
relating to the Highlands Project, you will "participate in making" or "influence,, that
decision.
3. Step Three: What are your economic interests?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generallS on the official or
on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
o
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment 2 of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a);
regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, parkrer, kustee,
employee, or holds any position of managgment (section s7103(d); regulation
18703.1(b));
2 An indirect investnent or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the offrcial, the official's immediate family, or theiragents own dtectly,
indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediati family,, is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.


o
a
o
a
File No. A-04-019
Page No. 5
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation
t8703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03(c); regulation 1 8703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 I 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family - this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87I03; regulation 18703.5).
Provided you have an interest of $2,000 or more in your residence, you have an
economic interest in this real property. You have not provided information regarding any
other economic interests. For purposes of this letter, we assume you have no other
economic interest relevant to the decisions you have identified.
4. Step Four: fs your economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?
One way in which real property is "directly involved" in a governmental decision
is if:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest,
or any part of that real property, is located within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the governmental
decision." (Section 187 0a.2@)(l).)
Your letter indicates that none of the Highland Project's proposed development
comes within one-quarter mile (approximately l32A feet) of your property line. Thus,
your real property will be indirectly involved in these decisions. 
3
5. Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is
indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material.
(Regulation 18705.2@)(1).) This presumption can be rebutted by proof that there are
specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision which make it reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on your home. (Ibid.)
3 Real property can also be directly involved in a decision in other ways specified in regulations
18704.2(a)(2)-(6). (See enclosed regulation 18704.2) None of these are applicable to your situation.


File No. A-04-019
Page No. 6
Examples of these specific circumstances that will be considered include, but are not
limited to:
"(A) The development potential or income producing
potential of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy,
intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits
of the nei ghborhood. " (Regul ation I 87 0 5.2(b)( I XA-C). )
6. Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the
governmental decision upon any of your economic interests will meet the applicable
materiality standard?
An effect upon economic interests is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is
a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
It is presumed that you do not have a conflict of interest arising from your
economic interest in your home. According to the facts provided, decisions concerning
the development plan presumablywill not have a material financial effect upon your
economic interest. However, you have stated that it is clear that the project will change
the area near your home from largely rural to largely residential. Consequently, it
appears the decisions you have identified may result in a material financial effect on your
residence so that this presumption is rebutted. Because the Commission does not act as a
finder of fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), it is ultimately
up to you to decide whether your facts are sufficient to rebut this presumption. If it is not
rebutted, you may participate in the decisions.
7. Step Seven: 66Public Generally" Exception
If you determine that the presumption of regulation 18705.2(b)(1) is rebutted, you
may wish to examine whether the "public generally'' exception applies.
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate if the "public generally''exception applies. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision upon a public official's economic interests is
indistinguishable from the effect of the decision on the public generally. (Section 87103;
regulation 18707(a).) Under this two-pronged exception, if a "significant segment"
(prong 1) of the public generally is affected by the governmental decision in


File No. A-04-019
Page No. 7
"substantially the same manner" (prong 2) as the decision affects the official's economic
interest, the official may participate in the decision.
Prong I -- Significant Segment
Real Property
Specific subdivisions of regulation 18707.1define what constitutes a "significant
segment." When the economic interest which gives rise to the conflict of interest is an
interest in real property, such as yours, the significant segment is defined as:
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all
homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or
the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners orhomeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency." (Regulation
I 8707. I O)(1)(BXi) - (ii).)
The facts show that the plan's effects are not localized to the area immediately
adacent to the project's proposed boundaries. Based on this information, it appears the
plan will affect a significant portion of the city in developing your area from rural to
residential.
We do not have facts to determine whether ten percent of all property owners, or
15,200 property owners, in the city will be'affected by this development. If ten percent
of all property owners, or 15,200 property owners, will be affected by the plan, then the
significant segment prong will be met. If not, then the exception is not applicable.
Prong Two - Substantially The Same Manner
After determiningthat a significant segment will be affected by a decision, it is
next necessary to evaluate whether the significant segment will be affected by the
decision in "substantially the same mannef'as you will be. Ifthe answer is'!es," t}ten
the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
and you may participate in the decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(a).) To assess whether a
significant segment will be financially af,fected in substantially the srrme manner, all
measurable effects from the decision must be identified. Without additional facts, we are
unable to determine whether this exception applies.
8. Step Eight: "Legally Required Participation, Exception
The "legally required participation" exception permits an official who is
otherwise disqualified from making a governmental decision to participate in the decision
when the official's participation is legally required. (Section 87101; regulation 18708.)
This exception (step eight of our standard conflict-of-interest analysis) is narrowly
construed and applies only when it is legally impossible for the decision to be made


File No. A-04-019
Page No. 8
without the participation of a disqualified official. Typically this exception is invoked
when, due to disqualification, an agency is unable to convene a quorum of its members.
In your case, you state that the city manager has requested your advice and
counsel in negotiating the proposed terms of the Highlands Project. This implies that it is
a request and not legally required, nor have you presented facts to indicate that there is no
alternate source of decision. Since this exception is narrowly construed and applies only
when a decision is legally impossible to make, it is unlikely that your participation as city
counsel is legally required.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
Rebecca Olson
Intem, Legal Division
Enclosure
RJojg

material financial effect on the economic interest of your principal residence. As long as

interest, the official may participate in the decision.
Prong I -- Significant Segment
Real Property
Specific subdivisions of regulation 18707.1define what constitutes a "significant
segment." When the economic interest which gives rise to the conflict of interest is an
interest in real property, such as yours, the significant segment is defined as:
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all
homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or
the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners orhomeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency." (Regulation
I 8707. I O)(1)(BXi) - (ii).)
The facts show that the plan's effects are not localized to the area immediately
adacent to the project's proposed boundaries. Based on this information, it appears the
plan will affect a significant portion of the city in developing your area from rural to
residential.
We do not have facts to determine whether ten percent of all property owners, or
15,200 property owners, in the city will be'affected by this development. If ten percent
of all property owners, or 15,200 property owners, will be affected by the plan, then the
significant segment prong will be met. If not, then the exception is not applicable.
Prong Two - Substantially The Same Manner
After determiningthat a significant segment will be affected by a decision, it is
next necessary to evaluate whether the significant segment will be affected by the
decision in "substantially the same mannef'as you will be. Ifthe answer is'!es," t}ten
the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
and you may participate in the decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(a).) To assess whether a
significant segment will be financially af,fected in substantially the srrme manner, all
measurable effects from the decision must be identified. Without additional facts, we are
unable to determine whether this exception applies.
8. Step Eight: "Legally Required Participation, Exception
The "legally required participation" exception permits an official who is
otherwise disqualified from making a governmental decision to participate in the decision
when the official's participation is legally required. (Section 87101; regulation 18708.)
This exception (step eight of our standard conflict-of-interest analysis) is narrowly
construed and applies only when it is legally impossible for the decision to be made
without the participation of a disqualified official. Typically this exception is invoked
when, due to disqualification, an agency is unable to convene a quorum of its members.
In your case, you state that the city manager has requested your advice and
counsel in negotiating the proposed terms of the Highlands Project. This implies that it is
a request and not legally required, nor have you presented facts to indicate that there is no
alternate source of decision. Since this exception is narrowly construed and applies only
when a decision is legally impossible to make, it is unlikely that your participation as city
counsel is legally required.

QUESTION: l. May you, as city attorneS render legal advice to the city council or staff
regarding the negotiation, drafting, and hearing process for the Moreno Highlands
Project, located about one-quarter mile from your residence?
2. May you advise the council at public hearings regarding modifications to the
land use entitlements for the phase of the project near your residence or for the Highlands
Project?

CONCLUSION: 1-2. Yes. Yourprincipal residence is located more than 500 feet from the
proposed boundaries of the Highland Project. It is presumed under the Commission's
regulations, therefore, that neither of these decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on the economic interest of your principal residence. As long as
this presumption is not rebutted, you will not have a conflict of interest concerning either
of these decisions, based on your principal residence.

May you, as city attorneS render legal advice to the city council or staff regarding the negotiation, drafting, and hearing process for the Moreno Highlands Project, located about one-quarter mile from your residence? 2. May you advise the council at public hearings regarding modifications to the land use entitlements for the phase of the project near your residence or for the Highlands Project? CONCLUSIONS 1-2. Yes. Yourprincipal residence is located more than 500 feet from the proposed boundaries of the Highland Project. It is presumed under the Commission's regulations, therefore, that neither of these decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the economic interest of your principal residence. I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations. iq9-5: File No. 4-04-019 Page No. 2 FACTS The City of Moreno Valley is a rapidly growing city of approximately 152,000 in population. It is approximately 50 to 60 percent developed, with large areas of undeveloped land on the city's east side. Your personal residence is located in a developed single-family tract surrounded by undeveloped fields. It is located slightly more than one-quarter of a mile from Redlands Blvd., the major north/south arterial street in the area. The land near your residential development contains a few large lot residential homes, a horse ranch, and undeveloped fields that have been 'odry-farmed" for a number of years. Most of the land has received land use entitlements as part of a specific plan ar'td a development agreement both approved prior to your appointment as city attorney and your purchase of your home. The development pdect, called "Moreno Highlands" ("Highlands Project') was subject to CEQA litigation at the time of its approval, and the environmental documentation was ruled to be legally adequate. However, the project has been dormant through the recessionary years. The master developer for the Highlands Project wishes to resurrect the project and make some changes to the specific plan, including the elimination of some land recently acquired by the State of California for additional wildlife preserve and adding some land recently acquired by the developer on the northeast corner of the project. He also wishes to reduce the density and total number of residential units, phase the project and amend and restate the development agreernent for the project. The Highlands project is an irregularly
